planting.note: Surface sow directly at or just after last frost into a disturbed, bare soil patch, as seeds require light to germinate and should not be covered.

directions: Sweet Everlasting is a native annual or biennial of dry, open fields and forest margins throughout eastern North America, beloved for its silvery-white, woolly everlasting flower clusters that emit an intensely sweet vanilla-like fragrance both fresh and dried. Direct sow onto the surface of well-drained, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun, pressing seeds lightly into the soil without covering them, as light is required for germination. Plants are drought-tolerant once established and thrive in poor soils where competition is low, making them excellent for dry wildflower meadows, roadside plantings, and naturalized areas. Cut stems just before the flower clusters fully open and hang upside-down to dry; the fragrance persists for many months in dried arrangements.

planting.note: Fall sowing into partially shaded, well-drained woodland soil allows natural stratification and reliable germination the following spring.

directions: Gronovius's Hawkweed is a native perennial of dry woodland edges and open forests across eastern North America, producing rosettes of distinctly hairy leaves and airy branching stems bearing small yellow dandelion-like flowers throughout mid- to late summer. It is one of the few native hawkweeds suited to the low-fertility, dry shade conditions under mature deciduous trees where many garden plants fail. Sow seeds directly in fall into a lightly shaded spot with well-drained, lean soil, scattering them onto a raked surface and pressing gently to ensure contact. Once established, plants self-seed modestly to form a low-maintenance ground layer that supports native specialist bees and other beneficial insects.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into dry, well-drained soil to allow cold stratification; seeds require a period of winter cold to break dormancy.

directions: Rush Skeletonweed is a striking native perennial of the central Great Plains, bearing slender, nearly leafless green rush-like stems that give the plant an unusual skeletal texture and provide excellent ornamental interest in dry and xeric garden designs. From June through September the stems are studded with cheerful lavender-pink, dandelion-type flowers that attract native bees and whose seeds are readily eaten by finches and other small birds. Sow seeds directly in fall into poor, dry, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; this species is exceptionally drought-tolerant and will decline rapidly in wet or clay-heavy conditions. It is best used in prairie restorations, gravel gardens, or alongside other low-water natives where its distinctive habit can be appreciated without overwatering from neighboring plants.

planting.note: Broadcast or drill seed in early to mid-fall into a prepared seedbed; the crop overwinters as a rosette and resumes rapid growth in early spring.

directions: Field Pennycress is a fast-growing winter annual cover crop naturalized throughout North America, valued for its ability to establish quickly in cool fall soils, suppress weeds through dense canopy coverage, and provide some of the earliest spring pollen and nectar available to emerging bees when almost nothing else is blooming. It is also being actively developed by University of Minnesota researchers as a high-oil oilseed crop for sustainable aviation fuel and other bioproducts, making it a cover crop with commercial upside potential. Sow seed in fall by broadcasting at 5-8 lb per acre into a firm seedbed; the plants overwinter as low rosettes and bolt to flower in April-May before ceasing growth. Terminate by mowing or rolling before seed set if you want to prevent naturalization, or allow some seed set to encourage re-establishment the following fall.

planting.note: Sow directly in fall into shaded, well-drained soil; seeds benefit from cool, moist conditions during stratification before spring germination.

directions: Leibnizia is a low-growing rosette perennial native to central Mexico, Guatemala, and the mountains of northwestern Chihuahua, prized among botanically curious gardeners for its remarkable dual flower strategy: showy open (chasmogamous) white-and-purple daisy flowers appear in spring, while a second flush of closed, self-pollinating (cleistogamous) flowers is produced later in the season without ever opening. This unusual adaptation allows the plant to set seed reliably even without pollinators, making it a resilient choice for dry shade conditions under trees or on north-facing slopes. Sow seeds in fall into humus-rich, well-drained soil in a sheltered spot with partial shade; established plants form tidy, attractive basal rosettes that spread slowly over time. It pairs well with other dry-shade natives such as woodland sedges and low ferns, requiring only occasional watering during extended dry periods once roots are established.
